page: UpCloud DEV-1xCPU-2GB is an entry-level virtual machine belonging to the Developer server family. It is provisioned with a single shared AMD EPYC 7542 vCPU operating at 2.0 GHz on the x86_64 architecture, featuring 1 physical core and 1 thread. The system is equipped with 2.0 GB of RAM and 30 GB of local SSD storage, running on the KVM hypervisor. A single complimentary public IPv4 address is included with the instance.

Performance benchmarks indicate that this server is designed for lightweight workloads. The shared CPU allocation results in a stress-ng single-core and multi-core score of 739.58, placing it in the bottom 10% of tested instances. Memory bandwidth is similarly constrained, with large-block read and write speeds measuring 9.08 GB/sec and 7.31 GB/sec respectively. However, the server demonstrates moderate capability in single-threaded gzip compression, achieving an average throughput of 24.3 GB/sec.

Given its resource profile and shared CPU architecture, the DEV-1xCPU-2GB instance offers a highly cost-efficient option for non-production environments. It is best suited for software development, testing, basic scripting, and running lightweight applications that do not require sustained high-performance compute. It is not recommended for database-heavy operations or production-level web serving due to lower database and static web serving benchmark results.
description: UpCloud DEV-1xCPU-2GB is an entry-level virtual machine from the Developer family, configured with 1 shared AMD EPYC 7542 vCPU, 2.0 GB of RAM, and 30 GB of local SSD storage. Operating on the x86_64 architecture via the KVM hypervisor, this server is designed for budget-conscious, non-production use cases. Benchmark results place its compute and memory performance in the lower tier, with a stress-ng score of 739.58 and a Passmark CPU score of 2028.76, though it maintains average single-threaded gzip compression performance at 24.3 GB/sec. This instance provides a highly cost-efficient environment for software development, code testing, lightweight scripting, and learning environments where dedicated CPU resources are not required.
